museum.name: Gaming Time Machine
museum.dek: The month when Lara Croft returned, GoldenEye kept four controllers warm, PC players watched the Quake II countdown, and the Christmas shelves began to feel heavy.
museum.curatorNote: This November exhibit is UK-aware rather than date-obsessed: exact day-level release dates are only used where multiple sources agree, and uncertain items are labelled as November or late 1997.

sections.title: News
sections.subtitle: A month of arrivals, aftershocks, and the particular UK sensation that the high street was setting its Christmas display early.

items.title: Tomb Raider II becomes the November object of attention
items.body: Core Design and Eidos put Lara Croft back into the centre of the PlayStation conversation. Modern databases often list 21 November 1997 for North America and Europe, while a contemporary UK business report says the game had launched in the US the previous week and had just reached the UK by 28 November.
items.certainty: verified month; exact UK day disputed
items.sourceNote: Wikipedia and Push Square list 21 November 1997; The Independent on 28 November 1997 says Tomb Raider II was released in the UK the previous day.

items.title: GoldenEye 007 becomes a Christmas multiplayer machine
items.body: Rare's N64 shooter was not new in November, but its UK momentum was unmistakable: a late-August release had turned into word-of-mouth, sleepovers, and four-player split-screen arguments before Christmas.
items.certainty: contextual momentum, not a new release
items.sourceNote: Nintendo Life and Giant Bomb list the UK/Europe GoldenEye 007 date as 25 August 1997; November entry describes continued holiday momentum.

items.title: Fallout settles into the PC RPG conversation
items.body: After its October launch, Fallout began to feel less like one more boxed RPG and more like a whispered recommendation: darker, funnier, and more willing to let a player make a mess.
items.sourceNote: Fallout release is sourced to October 10, 1997; this November note describes post-launch conversation rather than a new dated event.

items.title: Gran Turismo waits just beyond the glass in Japan
items.body: Polyphony Digital's Gran Turismo had not yet launched, but Japanese PlayStation anticipation was part of the late-1997 air. Its 23 December Japanese release would arrive just after this exhibit month.
items.sourceNote: Official Gran Turismo product pages list the Japanese release date as 23 December 1997.

items.title: Quake II approaches on PC
items.body: id Software's next shooter was still imminent in November. PC players were already thinking about OpenGL, deathmatch, hardware, and whether their machines were ready for December.
items.sourceNote: Quake II references list 9 December 1997 in North America and 12 December 1997 in Europe.

sections.title: Releases
sections.subtitle: Not a fake day-by-day calendar: a shelf record of what was newly arrived, freshly reviewed, or looming over November 1997.

items.title: Tomb Raider II
items.body: The month's headline retail object: Core Design's sequel put Lara Croft back into the high-street window and into the UK games press bloodstream.
items.certainty: verified month; exact UK day disputed
items.sourceNote: See news item source note; 21 November appears in many modern references, while The Independent implies a UK release around 27 November.

items.title: GoldenEye 007
items.body: A summer release that behaved like a winter system-seller. By November it was less a new game than a reason to ask for extra controllers.
items.sourceNote: UK/Europe release date listed as 25 August 1997; included for November momentum.

items.title: Fallout
items.body: Still fresh from October, Fallout occupied the PC shelf as the colder, stranger RPG next to brighter fantasy boxes.
items.sourceNote: Fallout release date is October 10, 1997; included as post-launch context.

items.title: Quake II
items.body: Not out yet, but close enough to bend magazine previews and PC upgrade fantasies around itself.
items.sourceNote: Quake II released in December 1997; this item is anticipation, not a November launch.

items.title: Gran Turismo
items.body: The Japanese launch was still weeks away, but the promise of car culture, licence tests, and real-world handling was becoming visible through import previews.
items.sourceNote: Official Gran Turismo pages list 23 December 1997 for Japan.

items.title: Christmas 1997 shelves
items.body: The release story was also retail: PlayStation breadth, N64 exclusives, and PC CD-ROM boxes fought for the same gift-list space.
items.sourceNote: Forbes, The Independent, and Eidos marketing coverage describe PlayStation/N64 competition, Christmas game promotions, and Tomb Raider II campaign pressure.

sections.title: Hardware
sections.subtitle: The November machine room: consoles under trees, PCs under strain, and accessories becoming part of the social ritual.

items.title: PlayStation as the safe Christmas bet
items.body: By winter 1997, PlayStation's catalogue depth made it feel like the high-street default: Tomb Raider II, Final Fantasy VII, demos, memory cards, and a wall of third-party boxes.
items.sourceNote: Forbes' 1997 PlayStation-versus-N64 coverage discusses PlayStation's software shipment advantage over Nintendo 64.

items.title: Nintendo 64 as the multiplayer argument
items.body: The N64's UK Christmas strength was social: GoldenEye, Mario Kart 64, Lylat Wars rumble, and the promise that four controller ports could turn one room into a tournament.
items.sourceNote: GoldenEye UK/Europe release date is sourced to August 1997; Lylat Wars/Rumble Pak context is sourced to October 1997.

items.title: PC CD-ROM as upgrade anxiety
items.body: A PC game box was still a checklist: processor, RAM, CD-ROM speed, sound card, DirectX, and increasingly whether your graphics hardware could do the future justice.
items.sourceNote: 3dfx/Glide and Quake II context support the late-1997 PC graphics-card pressure.

items.title: The magazine demo disc
items.body: For many UK players, a cover-mounted demo disc was a hardware-adjacent object: something the console or PC physically consumed, turning the magazine into a playable exhibit.
items.sourceNote: Official UK PlayStation Magazine listings and Edge issue records support the demo-disc/cover-disc context.

sections.title: Magazine Covers
sections.subtitle: The November shelf was a forecast: Lara, Bond, Final Fantasy, Quake II, and Christmas buying pressure compressed into cover lines.

items.title: Official UK PlayStation Magazine #25
items.headline: Final Fantasy VII, Tomb Raider II and demo-disc culture
items.coverImageAlt: Reconstructed cover image for Official UK PlayStation Magazine issue 25, November 1997.
items.curatorNote: A PlayStation newsagent object: Final Fantasy VII as cultural prestige, Tomb Raider II on the near horizon, and a playable disc as part of the purchase.
items.body: A PlayStation newsagent object: Final Fantasy VII as cultural prestige, Tomb Raider II on the near horizon, and a playable disc as part of the purchase.
items.sourceNote: Pyro Games lists Official UK PlayStation Magazine November 1997 issue 25 with Final Fantasy VII and Tomb Raider II tags.

items.title: N64 Magazine Issue 8
items.headline: GoldenEye, Yoshi, Diddy Kong Racing and the N64 winter
items.coverImageAlt: Reconstructed cover image standing in for N64 Magazine issue 8, November 1997.
items.curatorNote: The N64 press in November carried the afterglow of GoldenEye and the feeling that Nintendo's European Christmas had finally begun.
items.body: The N64 press in November carried the afterglow of GoldenEye and the feeling that Nintendo's European Christmas had finally begun.
items.sourceNote: Retromags and Magazines from the Past list N64 issue 8 with cover date November 1997.

items.title: GamePro November 1997
items.headline: Tomb Raider II, Final Fantasy VII, Oddworld
items.coverImageAlt: Reconstructed cover image for GamePro, November 1997.
items.curatorNote: A North American counterpoint to the UK shelf, but its cover lines mirror the same holiday pressure: Lara, Square, and cinematic PlayStation worlds.
items.body: A North American counterpoint to the UK shelf, but its cover lines mirror the same holiday pressure: Lara, Square, and cinematic PlayStation worlds.
items.sourceNote: Noble Knight lists GamePro November 1997 with Tomb Raider II, Final Fantasy VII, and Oddworld cover line.

items.title: Edge November 1997
items.headline: The waiting room: Gran Turismo, Quake II and Lara Croft
items.coverImageAlt: Reconstructed cover image for Edge, November 1997.
items.curatorNote: Included as a UK high-street placeholder for the premium-magazine mood: fewer cheats, more future tense, and a colder view of where games were going.
items.body: Included as a UK high-street placeholder for the premium-magazine mood: fewer cheats, more future tense, and a colder view of where games were going.
items.certainty: contextual magazine entry; issue-specific cover line not independently verified
items.sourceNote: Kept deliberately cautious: Edge issue 50 is verified for October; this November entry reflects UK magazine context rather than a confirmed exact cover scan.

sections.title: Online Life
sections.subtitle: Still noisy, still scheduled, still slightly magical when it worked.

items.title: Tomb Raider II hints travelled fast enough
items.body: The web was not yet the first place most UK players went, but walkthroughs, newsgroups, and fan pages could already make a new release feel mapped within days.

items.title: Quake II anticipation lived online before release
items.body: The PC shooter crowd used websites, FTP mirrors, server tools, and hardware talk as part of the waiting ritual. The game had not arrived yet, but the conversation had.

items.title: GoldenEye was mostly offline, socially online
items.body: Its multiplayer legend moved by word of mouth, magazines, school talk, and living-room sessions rather than internet matchmaking.

items.title: Demo discs were still faster than the modem
items.body: For many homes, the most reliable monthly download was the disc stuck to the front of a magazine.

sections.title: What It Felt Like
sections.subtitle: November 1997 as a month of wanting: wanting the sequel, the extra controller, the better PC, the right magazine, the Christmas answer.

items.title: The high street felt curated
items.body: HMV, Electronics Boutique, Dixons, WHSmith, and the local independent did not just sell games. In November, they arranged the future under fluorescent light.

items.title: Lara Croft felt bigger than the box
items.body: Tomb Raider II arrived with advertising weight, magazine gravity, and the sense that a British-made game could be a mainstream pop object.

items.title: The N64 defended itself socially
items.body: PlayStation had the wall of boxes, but the N64 had GoldenEye on a sofa with four people leaning forward.

items.title: PC players watched the horizon
items.body: Fallout was already installed, Age of Empires was still eating evenings, and Quake II sat just beyond November like a system requirement in human form.
